All kubectl commands fail with \\\"credentials\\\" error.\\n\\nThe cluster is a Rackspace Spot cluster (ORD region). The task description contained incorrect expiration information (reported 2026-04-26, actual expiration is 2026-05-01 22:37:44 UTC). Verified cluster access with kubectl get nodes and kubectl get pods -A — both commands returned successfully.\\n\\n## Retrospective\\n- **What worked:** Decoding the JWT token directly to verify actual expiration, rather than assuming the task description was correct.\\n- **What didn't:** Relying on the task description's expiration date without verification — it was 5 days stale.\\n- **Surprise:** The kubeconfig was already refreshed; the bead was created based on outdated information.\\n- **Reusable pattern:** Always verify token expiration by decoding the JWT payload before attempting a refresh — base64 -d on the middle segment contains exp timestamp.\",\"created_at\":\"2026-05-01T19:23:40.675855988Z\"}]}\n2\t{\"id\":\"armor-g9v\",\"title\":\"XML error responses don't escape special characters in the message field\",\"description\":\"When ARMOR proxies a B2 error whose message contains XML-special characters (e.g., '&' in 'Caps & Alerts page'), the writeError helper embeds the raw string directly into the <Message> element without escaping it. The resulting XML is malformed, causing clients that parse the error body (e.g., litestream's AWS SDK) to fail with a secondary XML parse error instead of a clean error code.\\n\\nRepro: trigger a B2 TransactionCapExceeded — ARMOR returns:\\n  <Message>...Transaction cap exceeded, see the Caps &amp; Alerts page...</Message>\\nbut the & is unescaped, breaking clients.\\n\\nFix: use xml.EscapeText (or encode via xml.Marshal) when constructing the Message field in writeError / writeErrorXML.\",\"design\":\"\",\"acceptance_criteria\":\"\",\"notes\":\"\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"priority\":2,\"issue_type\":\"bug\",\"assignee\":\"acb-20260424203315-0\",\"owner\":\"\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-24T21:32:37.536509237Z\",\"created_by\":\"coding\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-24T22:40:42.257302041Z\",\"closed_at\":\"2026-04-24T22:40:42.257229449Z\",\"close_reason\":\"done\",\"closed_by_session\":\"\",\"source_system\":\"\",\"source_repo\":\".\",\"deleted_by\":\"\",\"delete_reason\":\"\",\"original_type\":\"\",\"compaction_level\":0,\"original_size\":0,\"sender\":\"\"}\n3\t{\"id\":\"armor-hnq\",\"title\":\"Reduce B2 Class C transaction churn from /readyz canary\",\"description\":\"## Why\\nord-devimprint hit Backblaze B2's Class C (metadata) transaction cap.\\nDirect AWS CLI to B2 from inside the cluster confirmed:\\n  AccessDenied (ListObjectsV2): Transaction cap exceeded.\\n\\nThe biggest single source of Class C calls is ARMOR's own `/readyz`\\nhandler (internal/server/server.go:243), which on every readiness probe\\nruns:\\n    s.backend.List(ctx, s.config.Bucket, \\\"\\\", \\\"\\\", \\\"\\\", 1)\\nWith Kubernetes readinessProbe period=10s and N replicas, that is\\n6×N List calls per minute against B2 — roughly 17,000/day for 2\\nreplicas. B2's free Class C cap is 2,500/day; everything beyond that\\nreturns 403 AccessDenied, which our clients log as a credentials\\nfailure and our own readiness probe interprets as \\\"backend down\\\",\\nproducing a self-reinforcing degraded state.\\n\\n## Goal\\nMake the readiness probe correct without spending a B2 metadata\\ntransaction per probe.
